title: World Cup A Chance For Wissa To Show Toon Fans The Real Wissa
original_text:
When Newcastle United paid around £55 million to bring Yoane Wissa to St James’
Park, the expectation was clear: goals, energy, versatility, and the proven
Premier League quality he had displayed during his outstanding spell at
Brentford. Instead, his first season on Tyneside has been defined by injuries,
disrupted momentum and a struggle to establish himself in Eddie Howe’s starting
XI.

Now, however, comes the perfect opportunity for a reset.

The 2026 World Cup could be the stage where Wissa reminds Newcastle
[https://roundtable.io/sports/soccer/premier-league/newcastle-united] supporters
exactly why the club fought so hard to sign him in the first place.

Few players arrive at a major tournament with more to prove. Wissa’s Newcastle
career was knocked off course almost immediately after his move when a knee
injury suffered on international duty delayed his integration into the squad and
robbed him of valuable months at a crucial stage of his adaptation.

As a result, many Newcastle
[https://roundtable.io/sports/soccer/premier-league/newcastle-united] fans have
only seen flashes rather than the complete package. They have witnessed moments
of pace, intelligent movement and relentless pressing, but not the consistent
threat that made him one of the Premier League’s most dangerous forwards before
his move north. At Brentford
[https://roundtable.io/sports/soccer/premier-league/brentford], Wissa was a
player capable of stretching defences, attacking space behind the back line and
delivering goals in big moments. His reputation was built on reliability as much
as explosiveness.

The World Cup offers him a chance to showcase those qualities again.

Representing the Democratic Republic of Congo at their first World Cup finals
since 1974, Wissa will carry significant responsibility within a team looking to
surprise more established nations. The Leopards are unlikely to dominate
possession against opponents such as Portugal and Colombia, meaning transitional
attacks, work rate and clinical finishing will be vital.

Those circumstances suit Wissa perfectly.

He thrives when space opens up. He is at his best when defenders are forced to
turn and chase. More importantly, international football often rewards players
who can make decisive contributions from limited opportunities. That has long
been one of Wissa’s greatest strengths.

A strong tournament would not simply boost his reputation internationally. It
could completely change perceptions among Newcastle
[https://roundtable.io/sports/soccer/premier-league/newcastle-united]
supporters. Football fans are quick to forgive difficult beginnings when a
player demonstrates quality on a major stage. A couple of goals, a standout
performance against elite opposition, or helping Congo reach the knockout rounds
would instantly remind everyone that Newcastle did not sign a squad player—they
signed a forward who has repeatedly delivered at the highest level.

There is also a psychological aspect. Confidence is everything for attacking
players. After a frustrating campaign in black and white, a successful World Cup
could send Wissa back to Newcastle
[https://roundtable.io/sports/soccer/premier-league/newcastle-united] with
renewed belief, renewed fitness and renewed momentum. We have seen countless
players use international tournaments as launching pads for club success.

For Newcastle, that would be an ideal outcome. The club invested heavily because
they believed Wissa could become a key attacking weapon. The talent has never
disappeared; circumstances simply prevented supporters from seeing it
consistently.

This summer, on football’s biggest stage, Wissa has the chance to remind
everyone what made him worth the investment. If he delivers for Congo, Newcastle
[https://roundtable.io/sports/soccer/premier-league/newcastle-united] fans may
finally see the player they thought they were getting all along.
